result:
# The Weight of Almost

The house at the end of Caldwell Street had been empty for six years, which was long enough for the neighborhood children to have invented three separate ghost stories about it, but not long enough for anyone to have forgotten the woman who'd lived there.

Margaret Tierney had not been remarkable in any of the ways that typically make a person memorable. She didn't throw extravagant parties or maintain a garden that stopped joggers mid-stride. She wasn't beautiful in the way that invites narrative, nor was she unpleasant enough to generate the friction that keeps a person alive in local memory. She was, by most accounts, a woman who kept her hedges trimmed and brought a adequate casserole to the block party each September—always something with green beans, always slightly overcooked, always placed on the table with a kind of apologetic precision that suggested she knew it wasn't anyone's favorite but hoped it might be someone's second choice.

What made Margaret memorable was the way she left.

Not dramatically. Not in the middle of the night with a lover or a secret or a suitcase full of stolen money. She simply sold the house to a holding company, loaded a modest number of boxes into a rented van, and drove west. She told her neighbor, Denise Okafor, that she was going to "figure some things out," which is the kind of phrase that means everything and nothing simultaneously. Denise had nodded and said she understood, which was generous, because she didn't.

The holding company never developed the property. They never rented it. They simply held it, which is, after all, what holding companies do. And so the house sat, accumulating the particular sadness of places that were built to contain human life but have been emptied of it. Paint peeled in long strips like sunburned skin. The lawn grew wild in spring and died in patches through summer, creating a motley patchwork that resembled, if you squinted and were feeling poetic, a topographic map of neglect.

I know all of this because Denise Okafor is my mother.

---

I grew up across the street from Margaret's house, and I spent my childhood in the strange proximity of someone I barely knew but whose presence—and later, absence—shaped the geography of my daily life. When she was there, her house was simply a house. After she left, it became a landmark of a different kind: a monument to the possibility that a person could simply decide to stop being where they were.

This fascinated me at eleven. It terrified me at sixteen. At twenty-seven, which is what I am now, I find it mostly just sad.

My mother talked about Margaret more after she left than she ever had while she was present. This is, I think, a common phenomenon. We narrate people more urgently once they've exited the frame, as if the story might keep them tethered to us, or as if the telling might retroactively create a closeness that never quite existed in real time.

"She always seemed like she was waiting for something," my mother would say, usually while washing dishes, which was when she did her best philosophizing. "Not unhappy, exactly. Just... provisional. Like she was living a draft of her life and kept expecting the final version to arrive."

I think about that description more than I probably should.

---

I work at a public library in the city, forty minutes from the house where I grew up. My title is "Community Engagement Coordinator," which sounds like something generated by an algorithm trained on nonprofit job postings, and which in practice means I organize events, manage the library's social media presence, and spend a not-insignificant portion of my time helping elderly patrons connect to the Wi-Fi.

I love it in the way you love something that isn't what you planned—with gratitude cut by a faint, persistent guilt that you don't love it more.

What I planned was to be a writer. This is embarrassing to admit, not because there's anything wrong with wanting to write, but because wanting to write is so common as to be almost atmospheric, like wanting to travel or wanting to be understood. Everyone wants to write. The shelves of the very library where I work groan under the weight of all the wanting-to-write that actually made it to publication, and behind every published book stand a thousand abandoned manuscripts, and behind every abandoned manuscript stands a person at a dinner party saying, "I've always wanted to write a book."

I was going to be different, of course. I was going to be the one who actually did it. I attended a good MFA program. I wrote stories that my professors described as "quiet" and "controlled," which I chose to interpret as compliments but which I now suspect were polite ways of saying "nothing happens." I graduated. I moved to the city. I sat in coffee shops with my laptop open and a document blinking at me, and I discovered something that no one in my MFA program had thought to mention, which is that the distance between having written and writing is approximately the same as the distance between the Earth and whatever theoretical boundary marks the edge of the observable universe.

I could write when there was a deadline, a workshop, an audience of peers whose opinions I craved and feared in equal measure. I could write when writing was a performance of writing. But alone, accountable to no one, faced with the terrifying democracy of an empty page that would accept anything I put on it without complaint or encouragement, I found that I could not write at all.

Or rather: I could write, but I could not finish. I could begin. I was spectacular at beginning. I have, on a hard drive that I keep with the same grim devotion that a war veteran might keep a box of medals, forty-seven beginnings. Stories, essays, the first three chapters of two different novels. All of them have that quality my professors praised—quiet, controlled. All of them trail off at precisely the point where something would need to happen, where a character would need to make a choice that revealed them, where the author would need to commit to meaning something.

I think of Margaret Tierney. I think of living a draft.

---

There's a man who comes to the library every Tuesday and Thursday. His name is George, and he is eighty-one years old, and he reads exclusively about polar exploration. Shackleton, Amundsen, Scott, Franklin—the whole frozen canon. He has read every book we have on the subject multiple times. He requests interlibrary loans for obscure monographs. He once asked me to help him find the original Norwegian text of Nansen's *Farthest North*, not because he reads Norwegian, but because he wanted to "see what the words looked like."

I asked him once why polar exploration, and he said, "Because they went."

I waited for more, but that was it. They went. He said it with such simple admiration that I felt something shift in my chest—a rearrangement of some internal furniture that I'm still trying to identify.

They went. Not: they succeeded. Not: they survived. Many of them didn't. Scott died in his tent, eleven miles from a supply depot. Franklin's entire expedition vanished into the Arctic and was eaten, possibly in both the metaphorical and literal sense, by the landscape they'd tried to cross. Going was not, in many cases, distinguishable from dying. But George's admiration was not contingent on the outcome. It was for the moment of departure itself—for the decision to commit to a direction, knowing that the ice might hold you or swallow you and that either way, you would be irrevocably changed by the going.

I think about George a lot. I think about him when I open my laptop and close it again. I think about him when I read the news, when I watch people online arguing about things with a passionate certainty I can't seem to access. I think about him when I visit my mother and look across the street at Margaret's empty house and wonder where she went and whether the going was enough.

---

Last month, something happened that I'm still trying to understand.

I was closing the library on a Thursday evening—locking up, shutting down computers, doing the final walk-through to make sure no one had fallen asleep in the reading room, which happens more often than you'd think. In the back corner, on the floor between the stacks where we keep oversized art books, I found a notebook.

It was a cheap composition book, the kind with the black-and-white marbled cover that you can buy at any drugstore for two dollars. Someone had written in it in blue ink, in handwriting that was neither neat nor messy—the handwriting of someone writing quickly, for themselves, without concern for a reader.

I should have put it in the lost and found. That's what procedure dictates. Instead, I opened it.

It was not a diary, exactly. It was more like a collection of observations, each one dated but unconnected to the next. Some were only a sentence long. Others filled entire pages. They were written by someone who was paying very close attention to the world and trying, with evident struggle, to say what they saw.

*October 3: The woman on the bus was holding her coffee with both hands even though it wasn't cold. She was holding it the way you hold something when you need to feel that you're holding something.*

*October 7: There's a crack in the sidewalk on Elm where a dandelion has been growing since August. It's dead now but still standing. I don't know why this seems important.*

*October 15: I tried to call my father today and hung up on the second ring. The space between the first ring and the second ring contained my entire life.*

I sat on the floor of the library and read the whole notebook. It took about forty minutes. When I finished, I felt the way you feel after crying—emptied, tender, strangely clean—except I hadn't cried. I had just read the observations of a stranger who was trying to pay attention to being alive, and the attempt itself, the visible effort of it on every page, struck me as one of the most courageous things I'd ever encountered.

Not because the writing was beautiful, though some of it was. Not because the observations were original, though some of them were. But because someone had actually done it. Had sat down, alone, accountable to no one, and committed words to a page without knowing if they were good enough or true enough or enough enough. Had looked at the world and said: I see you, and here is my flawed, incomplete, possibly wrong attempt to say what I see.

They went.

I put the notebook in the lost and found. No one has claimed it. Part of me hopes no one will.

---

I've started writing again. I want to be careful about how I frame this, because I don't want to suggest that finding a stranger's notebook in a library was a magical catalyst that cured me of my paralysis and set me on a clear path toward literary fulfillment. Life does not work that way, and stories that suggest it does are, I think, a form of well-intentioned dishonesty.

What I want to say is smaller and less satisfying than that, which is probably a sign that it's true.

I've started writing again, and it is not going well. The writing is not quiet and controlled. It is loud and messy. It is the writing of someone who has spent years not-writing and is now over-writing to compensate, like a person who has been holding their breath and finally surfaces gasping, swallowing air and water in equal measure, drowning a little in the very thing that's saving them.

I write in the mornings before work. I write in a cheap composition notebook with a black-and-white marbled cover, because I am sentimental and because sentimentality, I have decided, is an underrated virtue. I write about my mother and her dishes and her theories about Margaret Tierney. I write about George and his polar explorers. I write about the house across the street, empty and holding, and about all the ways a person can be present in a place without ever quite arriving.

I don't finish things. I'm trying to make peace with that, or at least to stop treating it as a moral failure. Maybe some people are finishers and some people are beginners, and maybe the world needs both—needs the people who complete the expedition and the people who stand at the edge of the ice and describe, with terrified precision, what it looks like from here.

Or maybe that's a rationalization. Maybe I'm still living a draft.

But I'll tell you this: last Tuesday, George came in for his regular visit. He was carrying a new book—one he'd bought himself, not borrowed—about a failed 1897 attempt to reach the North Pole by hydrogen balloon. Three Swedish men launched from a Norwegian island, drifted for sixty-five hours, crashed on the ice, and spent three months trying to walk back to civilization. They didn't make it.

George set the book on the counter and looked at me with an expression I can only describe as delighted.

"They went," he said.

And I said, "Yeah. They did."

And it was not a grand revelation or a turning point or the climax of any narrative arc. It was a small moment in a public library between two people who were, in their own provisional ways, trying to be present for the fact of being alive. It was almost nothing.

But almost nothing is not nothing. And I'm learning, slowly, with great difficulty and no guarantee of success, that almost is where most of us live—in the space between the first ring and the second ring, between the draft and the final version, between the departure and the destination.

The house on Caldwell Street is still empty. My mother still talks about Margaret while washing dishes. I still don't know where Margaret went or whether she figured anything out.

But this morning, I opened my notebook and I wrote the first sentence of something I think might be true. And then I wrote the second sentence. And I didn't finish, but I didn't stop, and for now, that's close enough to going.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
